coredata.dc:title: Highly-flexible and -stable deep-ultraviolet photodiodes made of graphene quantum dots sandwiched between graphene layers
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Chan Wook
Shin, Dong Hee
Choi, Suk-Ho
coredata.dc:description: We first report highly-flexible and -stable deep-ultraviolet (DUV) photodiodes (PDs) by employing graphene (GR) quantum dots (GQDs) sandwiched between top/bottom GR layers on polyethylene terephthalates (PETs). Here, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane is inserted between the bottom GR and the PET substrate to enhance the bending stabilities without degradation or delamination by the chemical bonding. GQDs of ∼5 nm diameter are proved to be well formed on the bottom GR by various structural and optical analysis tools including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Raman scattering, and photoluminescence. The DUV PDs exhibit 10 photo-/dark-current ratio, 0.1 AW-1 responsivity (R), and 1.1 × 1013 cm Hz1/2/W detectivity at a wavelength of 254 nm. In addition, the R is reduced by only 13% even after 1000-times repeated bending tests at a bending curvature of 4 mm, and is almost consistent during the operations for 1000 h under ambient conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Graphene
Photodiode
Flexible
Deep-ultraviolet

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the configuration for characterizing a PD made of GQDs sandwiched between top/bottom GR layers on a PET substrate. (b) Low-magnification TEM and (c) HRTEM images of GQDs. The inset shows an image of crystalline lattice fringe. (d) Raman of spin-coated GQDs layer. (e) UV–visible absorption and PL spectra of GQDs in solution and spin-coated on the substrate. The inset shows a photography of the aqueous solution of GQDs exhibiting light yellow color. (f) AFM image and height profile of GQDs on a GR sheet. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) I-V curves under dark as well as under illumination at λ = 254 and 365 nm. (b) Bias-dependent PC/DC ratio and (c) responsivity at λ = 254 and 365 nm. (d) Reversible and regular photoresponse to pulsed light at different λ. Here, the voltage bias was fixed at +5 V.

(a) Reversible regular photoresponse to pulsed light at λ = 254 nm for various light intensities. Here, the voltage bias is fixed at +5 V. (b) Photocurrent as a function of light intensity under +5 V bias.

(a) Dark and photo I-V curves before and after bending tests at d = ∞, 8, 6, and 4 mm. The inset shows excellent flexibility of a PD. (b) Responsivity as a function of d. (c) Evolution of dark and photo I-V curves throughout 1000 bending cycles at d = 4 mm. (d) Responsivity as a function of bending cycle at a fixed d of 4 mm.

head.ce:title: Highly-flexible and -stable deep-ultraviolet photodiodes made of graphene quantum dots sandwiched between graphene layers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics and Institute of Natural Sciences, Kyung Hee University, Yongin, 17104, South Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Physics and Institute of Natural Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Two authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We first report highly-flexible and -stable deep-ultraviolet (DUV) photodiodes (PDs) by employing graphene (GR) quantum dots (GQDs) sandwiched between top/bottom GR layers on polyethylene terephthalates (PETs). Here, 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane is inserted between the bottom GR and the PET substrate to enhance the bending stabilities without degradation or delamination by the chemical bonding. GQDs of ∼5 nm diameter are proved to be well formed on the bottom GR by various structural and optical analysis tools including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, Raman scattering, and photoluminescence. The DUV PDs exhibit 10 photo-/dark-current ratio, 0.1 AW-1 responsivity (R), and 1.1 × 1013 cm Hz1/2/W detectivity at a wavelength of 254 nm. In addition, the R is reduced by only 13% even after 1000-times repeated bending tests at a bending curvature of 4 mm, and is almost consistent during the operations for 1000 h under ambient conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, photodetectors in deep-ultraviolet (DUV) wavelength (λ) range shorter than 320 nm are very attractive because they can be used in a variety of applications including chemical analysis, flame detection, ozone layer monitoring, and missile warning systems [1,2]. Traditional DUV or UV photodetectors are usually fabricated by using wide-band gap semiconductor such as ZnO, GaN, and AlGaN [3], but their high-quality thin-film manufacturing processes for the photodetectors are complicated and high-cost. Therefore, there is a strong demand for simple and low-cost manufacturing of the photodetectors. In recent years, carbon-based materials with high chemical stability, earthly abundance, and non-toxicity have emerged as attractive candidates for their applications in bio-imaging, catalysis, and optoelectronic devices [4–7]. Among them, graphene (GR) has received strong attention due to its unique electromagnetic, optical, mechanical, and thermal characteristics, originating from the two dimensionality [8–10]. However, GR is limited in its device applications due to the zero band gap nature. Recently, a lot of studies have been done for zero-dimensional GR quantum dots (GQDs) as a way to effectively open the band gap of GR [11–16]. The band gap of GQDs is tunable in the UV to DUV range based on quantum-confinement and edge effects [17]. In addition, the absorption range of the GQDs strongly depends on their size [16]. The smaller the size of the GQDs, the more suitable for their application in DUV photodetectors.

GR/GQDs hybrid structures are very attractive for high-performance detectors [18–22], but most of them are based on rigid substrates. By combining smaller-size GQDs and GR on polyethylene terephthalates (PETs), flexible DUV photodetectors with high photoresponse can be realized at λ < 400 nm. However, it is rather difficult for single-layer GR to be chemically bonded with a particular substrate [23]. Especially, the strong adhesion of GR on a flexible substrate is crucial for preventing them from being degraded/delaminated due to mechanical deformation during repeated bending tests. By inserting a 3-aminopropyl triethoxysilane (APTES) interlayer between GR and a substrate, their bonding strength was remarkably enhanced [24,25]. Here, we first report flexible photodiodes (PDs) made of GQDs sandwiched between GR layers on APTES/PET, showing strong and stable photoresponse in the DUV range. The GR/GQDs/GR PDs exhibit 0.11 A/W responsivity (R) and 1.1 × 1013 cm Hz1/2W−1 detectivity (D*) under applied voltage (Va) of 5 V at room temperature (RT). In addition, the PDs show excellent bending stabilities, maintaining about 87% of their initial R even after 1000 bending cycles, and the R is almost consistent under 1000-h exposure in air.

Single-layer GR layer was grown on a Cu foil by chemical vapor deposition under a flow of a gas mixture of CH4 and H2 at 1000 °C for 30 min. The APTES was spin-coated on a PET at 3000 rpm for 60 s and dried at RT. The APTES/PET substrate was then washed with methanol. Subsequently, the GR sheet was transferred onto the APTES-treated PET substrates of 1 × 1 cm2 size by using well-known poly(methyl methacrylate) supporting films. In this work, GQDs of 5 nm average diameter were purchased from Dotz Nano Ltd. 200-ml GQDs solution was dropped onto the whole surface of the GR (referred to as bottom GR), spin-coated at 1500 rpm for 1 min, and annealed at 100 °C for 1 min. This coating was repeated up to 12 times to enhance the density and thickness of the GQDs. Another GR layer of 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 size (referred to as top GR) was transferred on the GQDs/bottom GR to form the GR/GQDs/GR/APTES/PET structure. As a result, the bottom and top GR layers were prevented from being electrically contacted with each other. An Ag electrode film was deposited on the top GR to finalize the PD device of 0.25 cm2 active area.

The size and distribution of GQDs were analyzed by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F) with electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS) mapping capabilities. Absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) were measured to check the formation of GQDs. The PL spectrum was measured using a 325 nm HeCd laser excitation source with 3 mW power. Raman spectrum of the GQDs layer was measured in a micro-Raman spectroscopy system with a 1 mW-532 nm excitation source. The topographic image and height profile of GQDs were obtained in a noncontact mode of atomic force microscope (AFM, Park System XE-100). The atomic bonding states of the GR/APTES and GQDs were studied by x-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using Al ka line of 1486.6 eV. The Fourier-transformation infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained by using a Thermo Scientific Nicolet 5700 spectrometer. The work function of the GR layers was measured by Kelvin-probe force microscopy (Park systems, model XE 100). The calibration of the work function was done by using gold as a reference. Current-voltage (I-V) measurements of the PDs were performed by Keithley 2400 source meter. The dark current (DC) noise was measured with a dynamic signal analyzer coupled to a low-noise current preamplifier in the frequency range of 1–104 Hz. The photocurrent (PC) of the PDs was analyzed using UV lamp (UVITEC Ltd.) emitting λ of 254 and 365 nm and 450-W xenon light source connected to a monochromator in the λ range of 400–1000 nm. The incident light intensity was measured by a UV-enhanced Si photodiode.

Fig. 1 a shows a schematic of a typical sandwich-type GR/GQDs/GR/APTES/PET PD. To check whether the GR sheet and PET are chemically bonded by the APTES treatment, XPS peaks corresponding to C 1s and N 1s core levels were analyzed for the GR layer before and after the treatment (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). The C1s spectrum of the GR/APTES layer was resolved into five components of C-C, C-N, C-O, C=O, and C-C=O bonds at 284.8, 285.8, 286.2, 287.1, and 289.0 eV, respectively [26,27]. The main peak corresponding to a C-C bond was almost identical to that of graphite, indicating that most of the C atoms of the GR are arranged in a conjugate honeycomb lattice. A component of C-N-H bond was also found in N 1s XPS spectrum of the GR/ATPES layer. N elements in APTES can modify the band structure of graphene [28,29], but the gap opening due to the doping effect is known to be a few meV. This seems to be negligible for affecting the PD properties. Any of the N-related peaks were not observed in the untreated GR sheet, demonstrating the formation of a chemical bond between the GR and the PET by the APTES treatment. Fig. 1b shows a low-magnification TEM image of the GQDs used in this device. The diameter of the GQDs is distributed in the range of 3.8–7.8 nm, with a peak population at ∼5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1c reveals the crystallinity of GQDs with a lattice spacing of 2.08 Å, in good agreement with the (0110) plane of graphite [30,31]. In addition, carbon-K-edge EELS spectrum of the GQDs was peaked at ∼285 and ∼291 eV, corresponding to the π * and σ * states, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S2), further identifying the GQDs [16]. Fig. 1d shows Raman spectrum clearly peaked at ∼1360 and 1600 cm−1, corresponding to D and G bands, respectively, demonstrating the existence of GQDs, consistent with the previous reports [32,33].

Fig. 1e shows absorbance and PL spectra of the GQDs in solution and spin-coated on the substrate, peaked at 356 and 450 nm, respectively, as previously reported [34], indicating down-conversion behavior that absorbs light in the UV region and emits light in the visible region. These results also suggest that no structural variation of the GQDs occurs during the PD fabrication. In addition, FTIR and XPS spectra were measured (Supporting Information, Figs. S3a and b) to further characterize the GQDs. The FTIR spectrum in the range of 4000-400 cm−1 exhibited characteristic absorption bands corresponding to -OH, C=C, O-H, C-O, and C-O-C bonds at 3450, 1640, 1384, 1095, and 1045 cm−1, respectively. The C1s XPS spectrum was resolved into three peaks corresponding to C=C, C-O, and C=O bonds at 284.4, 286.5, and 288.2 eV, referring to sp2 carbon aromatic ring, hydroxyl functional group, and carboxyl functional group, respectively. These results suggest that oxygen-related molecules exist at the edge of the GQDs, i.e., the GQDs are high-quality. The thickness of the GQDs layer, extracted from the AFM images and height profiles (Supporting Information, Fig. S4), increased as the number (n) of spin coating increased. Pinholes were observed due to partial coverage of the GQDs up to n = 8. For n ≥ 10, the pinholes disappeared on the GQDs layer, whose thickness on the GR sheet was measured to be about 65 nm at n = 10, as shown in Fig. 1f (Supporting Information, Fig. S4f). In addition, any severe agglomeration of the GQDs was not observed for n ≥ 10. The root-mean-square roughness (Rq) of pristine GR was estimated to be ∼0.4 nm, consistent with the previous report [35,36], but after depositing the GQDs on the GR, the Rq increased to 3.4 nm due to the random-orientations of the GQDs.

The work function of the top/bottom GR layers were measured to determine the band diagram of the GR/GQDs/GR PDs (Supporting Information, Figs. S5a and S6). The GR layer exhibited n- or p-type characteristics due to the effect by the APTES or the GQDs, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S5a), consistent with the previous reports [37,38]. To further check this doping effect, we fabricated field effect transistors (FETs) containing pristine GR and GQDs/GR layers (Supporting Information, Fig. S5b). The ISD (source-drain current)-VG (gate voltage) curve of the GQDs/GR FET was shifted towards the positive voltage direction compared to the pristine-GR FET, indicating p-type characteristics of the GQDs/GR layer, consistent with the work function results and the previous report [38]. Based on these results, the GR/GQDs/GR PDs were fabricated at n = 10.

Fig. 2 a shows I-V curves of a typical GR/GQDs/GR PD under dark as well as under illumination (200 μW-cm−2) at λ = 254 and 365 nm. The I-V curves exhibit asymmetric properties with varying bias voltage. These transport behaviors have been discussed in detail for similar device structures in which both the top/bottom GR layers are slightly p-type, as previously reported by us [19]. The difference in the potential barriers between top/bottom p-/n-GR layers and GQDs results in the asymmetric I-V characteristics, as can be understood based on the energy-band diagram (Supporting Information, Fig. S6). Both electrons and holes photoexcited in the GQDs contribute to the total current under forward bias, but the photoexcited electrons contribute mostly to PC due to the lower potential barrier between the GQDs and the top GR [19]. In contrast, the higher potential barrier between the GQDs and the bottom GR limits the current from photoexcited holes under reverse bias [19]. These results suggest that the PDs are much more sensitive to the illumination under forward bias, resulting in higher PC under forward bias, as shown in Fig. 2a.

Fig. 2b shows the PC to DC ratios (on/off) of the GR/GQDs/GR PDs for λ = 254 and 365 nm, reaching maxima at bias voltages of approximately 3.2 and 4.1 V, respectively. Fig. 2c shows R values as functions of Va for λ = 254 and 365 nm. R was obtained by the equation: R = Jph/ILight, where Jph and ILight are PC density and incident light intensity, respectively. The R is maximized to 0.11 and 0.02 A/W at Va = 5 V for λ = 254 and 365 nm, respectively. The GR surface was not completely covered with GQDs up to n = 8, as shown in the AFM image results (Supporting Information, Fig. S4), resulting in ohmic curves due to the contact between the top and bottom graphene layers (Supporting Information, Fig. S7a). For n ≥ 10, the thickness of the GQDs layer as well as the R was almost saturated (Supporting Information, Fig. S7b). In addition, single-layer GR (SG)/SG (or bi-layer GR (BG)/BG) junctions without GQDs showed no PC, indicating the crucial role of the GQDs for the photoresponse (Supporting Information, Figs. S7c–d). On the other hand, the PDs are almost blind to the light in the visible to near infrared range (Supporting Information, Fig. S8), indicating their excellent selectivity in the DUV region. The PDs are also more useful at DUV than at UV, resulting from the high absorption of the GQDs at DUV, as shown in Fig. 1e.

Noise equivalent power (NEP) expressed as NEP = I
n /R [39], where I
n is noise current (Supporting Information, Fig. S9), was calculated from the R in Fig. 2c. D* is obtained from NEP based on the equation D* = (AΔf)1/2/NEP, where A is the effective area (0.25 cm2) of the PD in unit of cm2 and Δf is the electrical bandwidth in Hz. The D* is maximized to ∼1.1 × 1013 Jones at λ = 254 nm and Va = 5 V. This D * value is remarkably larger compared to previously-reported GR/GQDs hybrid structures [18,21,22,38], mainly resulting from the extremely-low DC, as shown in Fig. 2a. Fig. 2d shows regular and reversible behaviors of the photoresponse to pulsed lights at different wavelengths (see also Supporting Information, Fig. S10). It should be noted that the PDs are particularly sensitive to the 254-nm DUV light but are almost fully blind to the white light. This result further demonstrates the uniqueness of the GQDs useful for solar-blind DUV PDs. Based on the above results, additional photoresponse characteristics of the PDs were analyzed for λ = 254 nm.

Fig. 3 a shows power-dependent regular and reversible behaviors of the photoresponse to the pulsed light at λ = 254 nm. The DUV PDs are very sensitive even to very weak optical signals. The PC/DC ratio is ∼1.2 even at an incident light of 20 μW-cm−2. The PC increases almost linearly with increasing the light intensity, as shown in Fig. 3b, and the dependence of the PC on the light intensity satisfies the relation: PC ∝ Pα, where P is the light intensity and α is an empirical value related to the recombination processes of the photo-excited carriers. By fitting the curve base on the relation, α is calculated to be 0.99, as shown in Fig. 3b, indicating negligible recombination loss at the interfaces in GR/GQDs/GR layers [40]. The PD showed the response speed with rise (tr) and decay (td) times of 24 and 17 ms, respectively (Supporting Information, Fig. S11). The photoexcited electrons and holes in the GQDs migrate to the GR electrodes through the GR/GQDs interface as well as the GQDs layer, resulting in slower response speed compared to pristine GR [41].

To investigate the mechanical stabilities of the flexible DUV PDs, repeated bending tests were performed as functions of the curvature radius (d) (d = ∞, 8, 6, 4), as shown in Fig. 4 . The bending tests were done at a bending frequency of 0.5 Hz. Fig. 4a shows d-dependent DC and PC before/after bending tests. The R value is almost invariant under all the bending deformations, as shown in Fig. 4b. To further check the bending stabilities of the PDs, the bending tests were performed up to 1000 cycles, as shown in Fig. 4c. The R was maintained at ∼87% of the original value after 1000-cycles repeated bending tests under d = 4 mm, as shown in Fig. 4d. The R of the PD was also monitored for 1000 h at RT (Supporting Information, Fig. S12). During 1000 h, the R was almost consistent. The top GR layer possibly enhances the long-term stabilities by protecting the GQDs from the air.

Flexible GR/GQDs/GR/APTES/PET DUV PDs were successfully fabricated by employing GQDs of ∼5 diameter and p-/n-type GR sheets, and exhibited R and D* values of ∼0.11 A/W and 1.1 × 1013 cm Hz1/2/W at λ = 256 nm, respectively, comparable to those of the previously-reported GQDs/GR hybrid photodetectors on rigid substrates. The flexible DUV PDs also showed excellent bending stabilities, maintaining about 87% of their initial R even after 1000 bending cycles at d = 4 mm. Furthermore, the R was consistent under 1000-h operations in air. These simple, low-cost, flexible, stable, and high-performance GR/GQDs/GR/APTES/PET PDs are very promising for their applications in DUV optoelectronic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Science & ICT (NRF-2017R1A2B3006054).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.12.005.
